1.1: polumeros kai polytropōs — "at many times and in many ways" (polumeros: in many portions/parts; polytropōs: in many modes/manners): the adverbial pair describes the OT revelation's characteristic pluralism — different authors, genres, periods, and literary forms. The contrast is with the Son's single, definitive, eschatological speaking. Palaios (long ago) points to the temporal distance of the OT revelation; ep' eschatou tōn hēmerōn toutōn (at the end of these days) is the eschatological marker that the Son's speech is the final word in the series.
1.2: hon ethēken klēronomon pantōn — "whom he appointed heir of all things" (klēronomon: inheritor, heir): the appointment (ethēken, placed/set) precedes the creation in salvation-historical logic — the heir is appointed before the estate is created. Di' hou kai epoiēsen tous aiōnas — "through whom he also made the ages" (aiōnas: ages, worlds — not merely the temporal ages but the entire created order in its temporal unfolding). The Son as both heir and creator establishes his double relationship to the created order: he made it and he will receive it.
1.3:
apaugasma tēs doxēs kai charakter tēs hypostaseōs autou — "the radiance of his glory and the exact imprint of his nature" (apaugasma: radiance, effulgence — that which streams out from the source of light; charakter: the impression made by an engraving tool, the exact stamp/character — NT hapax): the two metaphors are complementary. Apaugasma describes the continuous streaming-out of glory from its source (like light from the sun — the sun's radiance is not the sun itself but is genuinely from it and conveys it). Charakter describes the exact correspondence between the impression and the seal — the Son bears the exact impression of the Father's hypostasis (substance, essential nature). Both terms are drawn from the Hellenistic philosophical and wisdom traditions.

pheron ta panta tō rhēmati tēs dynameōs autou — "upholding all things by the word of his power" (pheron: bearing, carrying, upholding — present active participle: continuously upholding): the Son's sustaining role in creation is present-tense and ongoing. Tō rhēmati tēs dynameōs autou (by the word of his power) — not by effort but by the same creative word that made all things.
1.4: tosoutō kreittōn genomenos tōn angelōn — "having become so much better than the angels" (kreittōn: superior, better — a key term in Hebrews used 13 times; genomenos: having become — aorist participle marking the historical event of the Son's exaltation): the Son's superiority is not merely ontological but the result of the exaltation after humiliation. The "becoming better" does not imply a prior equality with angels but the eschatological manifestation of what was always true. Diaphorōteron par' autous keklēronomēken onoma — "he has inherited a more excellent name than they" (keklēronomēken: perfect active — permanently inherited): the perfect tense indicates a permanent acquisition. The "name" is the Son-name (v5).
1.5: Hyios mou ei sy, egō sēmeron gegennēka se — "You are my Son; today I have begotten you" (Ps 2:7 LXX): the sēmeron (today) is the eschatological "today" of the resurrection-exaltation (Acts 13:33 applies this text to the resurrection). The divine begetting is not eternal generation (though that may be implied in the background) but the eschatological declaration of the Son's identity at the exaltation event. Egō esomai autō eis patera kai autos estai moi eis huion (2 Sam 7:14 LXX) — the Davidic covenant promise extended beyond its original Solomonic application to the definitive Son.
1.7: ho poiōn tous angelous autou pneumata kai tous leitourgous autou pyros phloga — "he makes his messengers winds, and his servants a flame of fire" (Ps 104:4 LXX): leitourgous (liturgical servants, ministers — the same word as the priestly and Levitical ministers): angels are characterized as servants/liturgists whose mode of being is as changeable as wind and fire — they serve but they do not reign. The contrast with the eternal Son (vv8-12) is the rhetorical point.
1.8: pros de ton huion, Ho thronos sou, ho theos, eis ton aiōna tou aiōnos — "But of the Son he says: Your throne, O God, is forever and ever" (Ps 45:6 LXX): the vocative ho theos addressed to the Son is one of the strongest direct divine-title applications in the NT. The Psalm 45 text, originally a royal wedding song, addresses the Davidic king as elohim (God/divine one) — Hebrews reads this as a divine address to the Son that confirms his divine status. Eis ton aiōna tou aiōnos (unto the age of the age = forever): the eternal duration of the Son's throne contrasts with the angels' servant role.
1.10: Sy kat' archas, Kyrie, tēn gēn ethemeliōsas, kai erga tōn cheirōn sou eisin hoi ouranoi — "You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands" (Ps 102:25-27 LXX): the citation addresses Kyrios (LORD = YHWH in the OT) but Hebrews applies it to the Son — a Christological reading of a Yahweh-text. The permanent stability of the creator (sy de diameneis = you remain) contrasted with the perishing creation establishes the Son's divine eternity over against the angels who, like the creation, are changeable.
1.13: pros tina de tōn angelōn eirēken pote, Kathou ek dexiōn mou heōs an thō tous echthrous sou hypopodion tōn podōn sou — "But to which of the angels has he ever said: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet?" (Ps 110:1 LXX): the rhetorical question clinches the argument — God has never addressed any angel with the right-hand session command. Heōs an (until) — the session is not permanent stasis but active waiting for the eschatological subjugation. The verse inaugurates the rest-and-waiting theme that will recur throughout Hebrews.
1.14: ouchi pantes eisin leitourgika pneumata eis diakonian apostellomena dia tous mellontas klēronomein sōtērian — "Are they not all ministering spirits sent out to serve for the sake of those who are to inherit salvation?" (leitourgika pneumata: liturgical/ministerial spirits — leitourgikos is the adjective of priestly/liturgical service; the angels as liturgical servants stands in contrast to the Son as liturgical high priest). The rhetorical question expects a "yes": all angels are servants-for-others, not seated lords.

2.1: dei perissoteros prosechein hēmas tois akoustheisin, mēpote pararrūomen — "we must pay much closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it" (pararrūomen: to drift past, to flow past — a nautical term for a ship drifting past its harbor because the pilot failed to steer properly): the image of passive drifting is the warning against neglect rather than active apostasy. The comparative perissoteros (more exceedingly) grounds the increased attentiveness requirement in the increased magnitude of the new covenant revelation.
2.3: pōs hēmeis ekpheuxometha tēlikaytēs amelēsantes sōtērias — "how shall we escape if we neglect such a great salvation?" (amelēsantes: having neglected, having cared nothing for — aorist participle): amelēo (to be unconcerned about, to neglect) implies passive indifference rather than active rejection — the warning is against those who simply do not take salvation seriously. The tēlikautēs (so great) grounds the impossibility of escape: the magnitude of the salvation corresponds to the magnitude of the consequence for neglecting it.
2.9: ton de brachy ti par' angelous ēlatōmenon blepomen Iēsoun dia to pathēma tou thanatou doxē kai timē estephanōmenon — "but we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death" (brachy ti par' angelous ēlatōmenon: made lower for a little — the Ps 8 phrase applied to the incarnate humiliation): the name Iēsoun (Jesus) appears here for the first time in Hebrews, positioned after the description of humiliation — the human name of the one who took the human condition. Doxē kai timē estephanōmenon (crowned with glory and honor) — the perfect passive participle indicates the permanent crown of the exaltation state.
2.10: eprepen gar autō... ton archēgon tēs sōtērias autōn dia pathēmatōn teleiōsai — "it was fitting for him... to make the founder of their salvation perfect through suffering" (archēgon: pioneer, captain, founder — one who leads the way, the first to go through a path that others will follow; teleiōsai: to perfect, complete, bring to the goal — the teleio- root pervades Hebrews): archēgos is not merely an author who initiates from a distance but a pioneer who personally traverses the route. The perfection through suffering is not moral improvement (the Son was not morally deficient) but the completion of the vocational path — the suffering was necessary for the high-priestly qualification.
2.11: ho te gar hagiazōn kai hoi hagiazomenoi ex henos pantes — "for he who sanctifies and those who are sanctified all have one source" (ex henos pantes: all from one — the henos is either masculine [one father, i.e., Abraham, or God] or neuter [one origin/source]): the shared origin establishes the solidarity that makes the Son's brotherhood genuine rather than merely analogical. Di' hēn aitian ouk epaischynetai adelphous autous kalein — "for which reason he is not ashamed to call them brothers" (ouk epaischynetai: is not ashamed — the social-shame context: the exalted one acknowledging his solidarity with the lowly).
2.17: hothen ōpheilen kata panta tois adelphois homoiōthēnai, hina eleēmōn genētai kai pistos archiereus ta pros ton theon eis to hilaskesthai tas hamartias tou laou — "therefore he had to be made like his brothers in every respect, so that he might become a merciful and faithful high priest in the service of God, to make propitiation for the sins of the people" (hilaskesthai: to propitiate, make atonement — the LXX verb for the priestly atonement act on the Day of Atonement [Lev 16:20 LXX]; used for covering/averting divine wrath against sin): the hilaskesthai debate centers on whether "expiation" (neutralizing sin's pollution) or "propitiation" (averting God's wrath) is primary. The LXX usage and the wrath-context of Hebrews favor propitiation, though the two are not sharply separable. Eleēmōn kai pistos (merciful and faithful) — the two qualifications for a legitimate high priest who genuinely represents the people before God.
2.18: en hō gar peponthen autos peirastheis, dynatai tois peirazomenois boēthēsai — "for because he himself has suffered when tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted" (peponthen: perfect passive of paschō = to suffer; peirastheis: having been tested/tempted — aorist passive participle): the perfect tense of peponthen (he has suffered, with the suffering-effect remaining) grounds the present capacity to help. The suffering is not merely past but bears on his present qualification. Boēthēsai (to come to the aid, to help — from boaō = to shout + theō = to run): to run to the cry of one who calls for help — the helping is urgent and responsive.

3.1: katanoeisate ton apostolon kai archierea tēs homologias hēmōn Iēsoun — "consider Jesus, the apostle and high priest of our confession" (katanoeisate: aorist imperative of katanoeō, to observe carefully, to fix one's attention on — the kata intensifies the observation): Jesus is called apostolos (apostle, sent one) only here in the NT — the term that the community uses for its own commissioned messengers is applied to the Son as the original sent-one from the Father. Homologias (confession): the common confession of faith that the community shares and that defines them.
3.6: Christos de hōs huios epi ton oikon autou — "but Christ is faithful over God's house as a Son" (epi ton oikon: over the house — the preposition epi with accusative indicates authority over): the Son's position is qualitatively different from Moses's en (within) the house — the Son is over it as the builder and heir. Hou oikos esmen hēmeis (whose house we are) — the community is identified as God's household; the ecclesiology is household-shaped. Eanper tēn parrēsian kai to kauchēma tēs elpidos mechri telous bebaian katáschōmen — "if indeed we hold fast our confidence and our boasting in our hope firm to the end."
3.13: alla parakaleite heautous kath' hekastēn hēmeran, achris hou to Sēmeron kaleitai, hina mē sklērunthē tis ex hymōn apatē tēs hamartias — "but exhort one another every day, as long as it is called today, that none of you may be hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" (apatē tēs hamartias: the deceitfulness of sin — apatē is deceit, delusion; sin is personified as a deceiver who leads the mind astray from reality): sklērunthē (be hardened — aorist passive subjunctive of sklērunō): hardening is a process that daily mutual exhortation resists. The "today" of Ps 95 is the present moment of the community's hearing — a kairos that closes when the heart hardens.
3.14: metochoi gar tou Christou gegonamen, eanper tēn archēn tēs hypostaseōs mechri telous bebaian katáschōmen — "for we have come to share in Christ, if indeed we hold our original confidence firm to the end" (metochoi tou Christou: partakers/sharers of Christ — metochos appears 6x in Hebrews; sharing/participation is a key Hebrews concept): hypostaseōs — the same word as in 1:3 (charakter tēs hypostaseōs); here it means the foundational confidence/assurance. The sharing in Christ is conditioned on maintaining the beginning (archēn) of that assurance to the end — the condition is not soteriological uncertainty but the community's perseverance in the faith-life.

4.12: zōn gar ho logos tou theou kai energēs kai tomōteros hyper pasan machairan distomos kai diiknoumenos achri merismou psychēs kai pneumatos, harmōn te kai myelōn, kai kritikos enthumsōeōn kai ennoiōn kardias — "for the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and of spirit, of joints and of marrow, and discerning the thoughts and intentions of the heart": zōn (living — present active participle: the word has continuous vitality, not static inscription); energēs (active, effective, at work — same root as energeō); tomōteros hyper pasan machairan distomos (sharper than every two-edged sword — tomōteros: comparative of tomos, sharp): the word's penetrating power exceeds the finest blade. Merismou psychēs kai pneumatos (division of soul and spirit) — not a technical trichotomy but the penetration of the innermost human reality; psychē and pneuma as the two terms for the inner life. Kritikos (discerning, able to judge — the root of krisis): the word not only penetrates but evaluates what it finds.
4.13: kai ouk estin ktisis aphanēs enōpion autou, panta de gymna kai tetrachēlismena tois ophthalmois autou, pros hon hēmin ho logos — "and no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account" (gymna kai tetrachēlismena: naked and exposed/laid bare — tetrachēlismenā is a NT hapax from trachēlos = neck; the image may be of an animal with its neck bent back for slaughter, fully exposed; or a wrestler pinned with head forced back): the divine omniscience before which all creatures are transparent is the basis of the word's discerning power.
4.15: ou gar echomen archierea mē dynamenon sympathēsai tais astheneiais hēmōn, pepeirasmenon de kata panta kath' homoiotēta chōris hamartias — "for we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin" (sympathēsai: to suffer-with, to feel-with — syn + pathein; not merely intellectual comprehension but shared experience): pepeirasmenon (having been tested/tempted — perfect passive participle: permanently in the state of having been tested — the testing is a completed historical event with permanent relevance): kath' homoiotēta (according to likeness, in the same way): the testing was genuinely analogous to human experience. Chōris hamartias (apart from sin): the crucial qualification — tested in every way but without sinning; not "unable to sin" as an ontological constraint but "having withstood every test" as a historical fact.

5.1: pas gar archiereus ex anthrōpōn lambanomenos hyper anthrōpōn kathistatai ta pros ton theon — "for every high priest chosen from among men is appointed to act on behalf of men in relation to God" (ex anthrōpōn lambanomenos: taken from among human beings — the high priest's humanity is a qualification, not a limitation): ta pros ton theon (things toward God — the priestly representative function): the preposition pros indicates directional orientation; the priest stands between human beings and God, facing toward God on their behalf. The two qualifications (v1: chosen from humans; v4: called by God) define the legitimate priesthood.
5.2: metriopathein dynatos tois agnoousin kai planōmenois, epei kai autos perikeitai astheneian — "he can deal gently with the ignorant and wayward, since he himself is beset with weakness" (metriopathein: to moderate one's feelings, to deal with measured emotion — a Stoic philosophical term for the appropriate emotional response between apathy and passion): the high priest's capacity for gentle response (metriopathein) is grounded in his own weakness-experience. The contrast with the Son's sympathy (4:15) is instructive: the Levitical priest sympathizes because he is himself weak; the Son sympathizes because he has been tested.
5.7: hos en tais hēmerais tēs sarkos autou deēseis te kai hiketerias pros ton dynamenon sōzein auton ek thanatou meta kraugēs ischyras kai dakryōn prosenenkas kai eisakoustheis apo tēs eulabeias — "in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence" (hiketerias: supplications, olive-branch petitions — the formal term for desperate appeal to a superior; the word is used in Gk papyri for formal petition): the Gethsemane prayer is described without being cited. Eisakoustheis apo tēs eulabeias — "heard because of his reverence" (eulabeia: godly fear, reverent awe — not anxiety but the reverent-careful posture before God): the prayer was heard — not by removal of death but by resurrection through death.
5.8: kaiper ōn huios, emathen aph' hōn epathen tēn hypakoēn — "although he was a Son, he learned obedience through what he suffered" (emathen aph' hōn epathen: learned from/out of what he suffered — the deliberate Greek wordplay on emathen [learned, aorist of manthanō] and epathen [suffered, aorist of paschō]; the rhyme and etymology emphasize the connection between suffering and learning): the learning is not correcting a moral deficiency but the vocational acquisition of lived obedience — the Son did not know obedience by abstract divine knowledge but through the experiential path of incarnate life. Kaiper ōn huios (although being Son): the divine sonship does not exempt from the learning-through-suffering path; it is the Son who walks this path.
5.9: kai teleiōtheis egeneto pasin tois hypakouousin autō aitios sōtērias aiōniou — "and being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him" (teleiōtheis: having been made perfect/complete/brought to the goal — aorist passive participle): the perfection (teleiōsis) through suffering and obedience is the high-priestly qualification. Aitios sōtērias aiōniou (cause/source/author of eternal salvation) — the aitios (cause, responsible party) is a philosophical and legal term; the Son is the efficient cause of the salvation that is aiōnios (eternal, age-spanning — not merely long-lasting but of the quality of the coming age).
5.11: peri hou polys hēmin ho logos kai dysermēneutos legein, epei nōthroi gegonate tais akoais — "about this we have much to say, and it is hard to explain, since you have become dull of hearing" (nōthroi tais akoais: dull in the ears/hearing — nōthros: sluggish, slow; the same word in 6:12 for sluggishness in faith): the pedagogical self-interruption — the author acknowledges difficulty in communicating and attributes it to the community's spiritual torpor, not the topic's intrinsic inaccessibility. The Melchizedek exposition that follows is difficult not because the truth is obscure but because the community lacks the spiritual attentiveness to receive it.

6.1: dio aphentes ton tēs archēs tou Christou logon epi tēn teleiotēta pherōmetha — "therefore let us leave the elementary doctrine of Christ and go on to maturity" (tēs archēs tou Christou logon: the word of the beginning of Christ — the foundational message; teleiotēta: maturity, completeness — the teleio- root again): aphentes (having left — aorist participle: a completed departure) and pherōmetha (let us be carried, be swept along toward — the passive suggests the Spirit's movement): the author does not call the foundation worthless but insists on building on it rather than always re-laying it. The six foundational items (repentance, faith, baptisms, hand-laying, resurrection, eternal judgment) represent Jewish catechesis that the community has mastered.
6.4: adynaton gar tous hapax phōtisthentas, geusamenous te tēs dōreas tēs epouraniou kai metochous genēthentas pneumatos hagiou kai kalon geusamenous theou rhēma dynameis te mellontos aiōnos, kai parapersontas, palin anakainizein eis metanoian — "for it is impossible, in the case of those who have once been enlightened, who have tasted the heavenly gift, and have shared in the Holy Spirit, and have tasted the goodness of the word of God and the powers of the age to come, and then have fallen away, to restore them again to repentance" (adynaton: impossible — the strong impossibility claim): the participles describe genuine spiritual experience: phōtisthentas (enlightened — a NT term for baptism), geusamenous (having tasted — genuine experiential contact, not merely intellectual acquaintance), metochous genēthentas pneumatos hagiou (having become sharers in the Holy Spirit). Parapersontas (having fallen alongside, having fallen away — the para-compound: falling beside the path): the cause of the impossibility (v6): "since they are crucifying once more the Son of God to their own harm and holding him up to contempt."
6.13: tō gar Abraam epangeilamenos ho theos, epei kat' oudenos eichen meizōnos omosai, ōmosen kath' heautou — "for when God made a promise to Abraham, since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself" (epei kat' oudenos eichen meizōnos omosai: since by no one greater he had to swear — the divine self-swearing is necessitated by the absence of a greater authority): human oaths invoke a greater authority to confirm what is said; God, having no greater authority, swears by his own name. The logic establishes the absolute certainty of the divine promise.
6.17: en hō perissoteron boulomenos ho theos epideixai tois klēronōmois tēs epangelias to ametatheton tēs boulēs autou emesiteusen horkō — "so when God desired to show more convincingly to the heirs of the promise the unchangeable character of his purpose, he guaranteed it with an oath" (boulomenos: desiring, wishing — deliberate divine will; ametatheton tēs boulēs autou: the unalterableness/immutability of his purpose — ametathetos: unable to be moved from, irrevocable): the divine oath is not required for God's truthfulness but is given for the benefit of the heirs — the double guarantee (promise + oath) gives maximum assurance to those who need confidence.
6.19: hēn hōs ankyran echomen tēs psychēs asphalē te kai bebaian kai eisercomenēn eis to esōteron tou katapetasmatos — "we have this as a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul, a hope that enters into the inner place behind the curtain" (ankyran tēs psychēs: anchor of the soul — the nautical metaphor of anchor applied to eschatological hope: ancient anchors were dropped into a safe harbor to hold the ship against current and wind; hope in the heavenly sanctuary holds the soul against the currents of the present age): asphalē te kai bebaian (sure and steadfast) — two terms of reliability. Eisercomenēn eis to esōteron tou katapetasmatos (entering into the inner sanctuary behind the curtain) — hope penetrates the sanctuary barrier where Jesus the forerunner has already entered.

7.2: hō kai dekatēn apo pantōn emerisen Abraam, prōton men hermēneuomenos basileus dikaiosynēs, epeita de kai basileus Salēm, ho estin basileus eirēnēs — "and to him Abraham apportioned a tenth part of everything. He is first, by translation of his name, king of righteousness, and then he is also king of Salem, that is, king of peace" (basileus dikaiosynēs: king of righteousness — Melchizedek = melek [king] + tsedeq [righteousness]): the etymology-based typology: the name's meaning is part of the type's significance. Basileus Salēm = king of Shalom (peace) — two royal qualities (righteousness and peace) that characterize the Son's rule (cf. Ps 72:1-3, 7; Isa 9:6-7).
7.3: apator, amētor, agenealogētos, mēte archēn hēmerōn mēte zōēs telos echōn — "without father, without mother, without genealogy, having neither beginning of days nor end of life" (apator, amētor, agenealogētos: without father, without mother, without genealogy — three privative adjectives): the crucial interpretive note: these predicates apply not to Melchizedek's biographical reality but to what the biblical text says about him. The Genesis narrative provides no genealogy, no birth, no death for Melchizedek — the text's silence is treated as typologically significant, not as historical information. Aphomoiōmenos de tō huiō tou theou (made like the Son of God) — Melchizedek is made-like the Son, not the reverse; the Son is the archetype, Melchizedek the type.
7.11: Ei men oun teleiōsis dia tēs Leuitikēs hierōsynēs ēn... ti eti chreia kata tēn taxin Melchisedek heteron anistasthai hierea — "now if perfection had been attainable through the Levitical priesthood... what further need would there have been for another priest to arise according to the order of Melchizedek?" (teleiōsis: perfection, completion — bringing to the goal; the subjunctive ēn in a contrary-to-fact conditional: perfection was not attained through the Levitical system): the argument from the existence of Ps 110:4 — if the Levitical system sufficed, God would not have promised a different kind of priest. The existence of the promise proves the insufficiency of the existing system.
7.16: hos ou kata nomon entolēs sarkikēs gegonen alla kata dynamin zōēs akatalytou — "who has become a priest, not on the basis of a legal requirement concerning bodily descent, but by the power of an indestructible life" (entolēs sarkikēs: fleshly/bodily commandment — the Levitical requirement of physical descent from Aaron; dynamin zōēs akatalytou: the power of an indestructible life — akatalutos: unable to be dissolved, indestructible; zōē not merely biological life but the life of the resurrection-age): the Melchizedekian priesthood is grounded in a different ontological category — not hereditary biology but the indestructible life that overcame death.
7.22: kata tosouton kai kreittōnos diathēkēs gegonen engyos Iēsous — "this makes Jesus the guarantor of a better covenant" (engyos: guarantor, surety, pledge-person — the one who puts himself up as security for another's debt or obligation; a legal-commercial term): Jesus is the covenant's guarantor, not merely its mediator. Kreittōnos diathēkēs (of a better covenant) — the first use of the "better covenant" language that will be elaborated in ch8. Diathēkē can mean both "covenant" (Hebraic-LXX usage) and "will/testament" (Greek usage); Hebrews exploits the ambiguity in 9:15-17.
7.25: hothen kai sōzein eis to panteles dynatai tous proserchomenous di' autou tō theō, pantote zōn eis to entynchanein hyper autōn — "consequently, he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them" (eis to panteles: to the uttermost, completely, for all time — panteles combines the ideas of completeness and duration): the interceding high priest who always lives is the basis of the complete salvation — his permanence guarantees the completeness of the benefit. Entynchanein hyper autōn (to intercede for them — entynchanō: to meet/approach someone on behalf of another; the present infinitive indicates continuous intercession).
7.26: toioutos gar hēmin eprepen archiereus, hosios, akakos, amiantos, kechōrismenos apo tōn hamartōlōn, kai hypsēloteros tōn ouranōn genomenos — "for it was indeed fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens" (hosios: devout, holy in relation to God; akakos: without evil, guileless; amiantos: undefiled, unstained; kechōrismenos apo tōn hamartōlōn: having been separated from sinners — the perfect participle indicates the permanent separation accomplished at the exaltation): the five adjectives form an ascending scale of purity culminating in the spatial exaltation above the heavens — the highest conceivable point of separation from the earthly-sinful realm.

8.1: kephalaion de epi tois legomenois, toiouton echomen archierea, hos ekathisen en dexia tou thronou tēs megalōsynēs en tois ouranois — "now the point in what we are saying is this: we have such a high priest, one who is seated at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven" (kephalaion: the main point, summary, head of the argument — not merely a summary but the key point that the whole argument has been building toward): tēs megalōsynēs (of the Majesty): a circumlocution for God, common in Jewish reverence-language. Ekathisen (sat down — aorist: the completed seating): the seated high priest, the completed work.
8.5: hoitines hypodeigmati kai skia latreuousin tōn epouraniōn, kathōs kechrēmatistai Mōysēs mellōn epitelein tēn skēnēn — "who serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things. For when Moses was about to erect the tent, he was instructed by God, saying: See that you make everything according to the pattern that was shown you on the mountain" (hypodeigmati kai skia: copy/example and shadow — both terms indicate derivativeness: the earthly sanctuary is not the original but its earthly reproduction; kechrēmatistai: has been warned/instructed by divine oracle — the same verb used for Joseph being warned in a dream, Matt 2:12): the divine instruction proves the earthly sanctuary is intentionally modeled on a heavenly reality.
8.6: nyn de diaphorōteras tetychēken leitourgias, hosō kai kreittōnos estin diathēkēs mesitēs, hētis epi kreittōsi epangeliais nenomothetētai — "but as it is, Christ has obtained a ministry that is as much more excellent than the old as the covenant he mediates is better, since it is enacted on better promises" (diaphorōteras leitourgias tetychēken: has obtained a more excellent ministry — diaphoros: different, superior; mesitēs: mediator, middle-person between two parties; nenomothetētai: has been legislated/established — the perfect passive indicates the settled legal establishment of the new covenant): the comparative structure: as much better as the covenant is better, as much as the promises are better — a chain of comparatives.
8.8: mempthomenos gar autous legei, Idou, hēmerai erchontai, legei Kyrios — "for he finds fault with them when he says: Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord. (mempthomenos autous: finding fault with them — the "them" are the people who made the first covenant inadequate by their failure): the author's reading of Jer 31 is that the new covenant promise itself implies a critique of the old: God would not promise a new covenant if the old had been without fault. The Jeremiah citation frames the promise as a divine critique-and-solution to the first covenant's demonstrated inadequacy.
8.9: hoti ouk estai kata tēn diathēkēn hēn epoiēsa tois patrasin autōn en hēmera epilabomenou mou tēs cheiros autōn exagagein autous ek gēs Aigyptou, hoti autoi ouk enemeinan en tē diathēkē mou, kagō ēmelēsa autōn, legei Kyrios — "not like the covenant that I made with their fathers on the day when I took them by the hand to bring them out of the land of Egypt. For they did not continue in my covenant, and so I showed no concern for them" (ouk enemeinan en tē diathēkē: they did not remain in the covenant — the first covenant's failure is not God's failure of design but Israel's failure of faithfulness; kagō ēmelēsa autōn: and I showed no concern for them — the divine response is corresponding covenant-withdrawal).
8.13: en tō legein Kainēn pepalaiōken tēn prōtēn; to de palaioumenon kai gēraskon engys aphanismou — "in speaking of a new covenant, he makes the first one obsolete. And what is becoming obsolete and growing old is ready to vanish away" (pepalaiōken: has made old — perfect active: the declaration of newness accomplished the obsolescence of the old): the kainēn (new — qualitatively new, not just recent) is the key: when Scripture uses kainēn, it implies the oldness of what it replaces. Engys aphanismou (near disappearance) — the present tense palaioumenon and gēraskon (becoming old, aging) suggest the process was underway at the time of writing; the Jerusalem temple's destruction (70 CE) would be the visible completion.

9.5: hyperanō de autēs Cheroubein doxēs kataskiazonta to hilastērion — "above it were the cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat" (Cheroubein doxēs: cherubim of glory — the genitive doxēs is a genitive of quality: cherubim characterized by glory, glory-cherubim; kataskiazonta: overshadowing, spreading wings over — the cherubim whose wings spread over the mercy seat [Exod 25:20] are the guardians of the divine presence): hilastērion: the mercy seat (kappōret) where the high priest sprinkled blood on the Day of Atonement — the place of atonement/propitiation. The same word in Rom 3:25 describes Christ as the hilastērion.
9.7: eis de tēn deuteran hapax tou eniautou monos ho archiereus, ou chōris haimatos, ho prospherei hyper heautou kai tōn tou laou agnoēmatōn — "but into the second only the high priest goes, and he but once a year, and not without taking blood, which he offers for himself and for the unintentional sins of the people" (hapax tou eniautou: once of the year, once per year — the hapax (once) anticipates the contrast with Christ's single sacrifice: the Levitical once-per-year vs. the Christological once-for-all; agnoēmatōn: unintentional sins, inadvertent transgressions — from agnoeo, to be ignorant of; the Levitical atonement covered inadvertent sins specifically, raising the question of intentional sins).
9.11: Christos de paragenomenos archiereus tōn mellontōn agathōn dia tēs meizōnos kai teleiotera skēnēs ou cheiropioētou, tout' estin ou tautēs tēs ktiseōs — "but when Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come, then through the greater and more perfect tent not made with hands, that is, not of this creation, he entered once for all into the holy places" (tōn mellontōn agathōn: of the good things that have come/are coming — the manuscript tradition is divided between mellontōn [coming/future] and genomenōn [having come/past]; both are theologically defensible): ou cheiropioētou (not made by hands — a technical term for that which belongs to a different ontological order, not constructed by human craft): ou tautēs tēs ktiseōs (not of this creation) — the parenthetical identification of the greater tent as belonging to a different created order from the earthly sanctuary.
9.12: oude di' haimatos tragōn kai moschōn dia de tou idiou haimatos eisēlthen ephapax eis ta hagia aiōnian lytrōsin heuramenos — "he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption" (ephapax: once for all — the compound of epi + hapax, emphasizing the finality and non-repeatability; aiōnian lytrōsin heuramenos: having found/secured eternal redemption — lytrōsis: the act of ransoming/redeeming, from lytron = ransom price): the contrast is between the animal blood that enables the annual temple ritual and the self-blood that enables the once-for-all heavenly entry. The perfect result (heuramenos = having found, secured) is permanent.
9.15: kai dia touto diathēkēs kainēs mesitēs estin, hopōs thanatou genomenou eis apolutrōsin tōn epi tē prōtē diathēkē parabaseōn, tēn epangelian labōsin hoi keklēmenoi tēs aiōniou klēronomias — "therefore he is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance, since a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions committed under the first covenant" (apolutrōsin tōn... parabaseōn: redemption-from transgressions — the apo-compound: ransom that moves someone away from the enslaving situation; parabaseōn: transgressions, willful crossings of the boundary, deliberate violations — in contrast to the agnoēmatōn of v7): the death redeems backward (from transgressions under the first covenant) as well as forward (securing the eternal inheritance).
9.22: kai schedon en haimati panta katharizatai kata ton nomon kai chōris haimatekcysias ou ginetai aphesis — "indeed, under the law almost everything is purified with blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins" (schedon: almost, nearly — the qualification acknowledges that a few Levitical purifications used water or fire rather than blood [Num 31:23; Lev 15:10-11]; the general principle holds): chōris haimatekcysias ou ginetai aphesis (without blood-shedding, forgiveness does not occur): haimatekcysia (blood-shedding, bloodshed): a rare compound found only here in the NT; the rule is absolute within the framework of the argument — atonement requires life given (Lev 17:11).
9.24: ou gar eis cheiropioēta eisēlthen hagia Christos, antitypa tōn alēthinōn, all' eis auton ton ouranon, nyn emphanisthēnai tō prosōpō tou theou hyper hēmōn — "for Christ has entered, not into holy places made with hands, which are copies of the true things, but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf" (antitypa tōn alēthinōn: antitypes/copies of the true things — antitypon: the corresponding impression, the copy-form that corresponds to its type): the terminology is inverted from usual typological language: normally the OT type corresponds to the NT antitype; here the earthly sanctuary is the antitypon (the copy, the corresponding-form) of the heavenly original. Nyn emphanisthēnai (now to appear — the present emphasis): Christ is currently appearing in God's presence for the community's benefit.
9.28: houtōs kai ho Christos hapax prosenechtheis eis to pollōn anenenkein hamartias, ek deuterou chōris hamartias ophthēsetai tois auton apekdechomenois eis sōtērian — "so Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many, will appear a second time, not to deal with sin but to save those who are eagerly waiting for him" (hapax prosenechtheis: having been offered once — the once-for-all offering is complete; eis to pollōn anenenkein hamartias: to bear the sins of the many — anaphero: to carry up, to bear [as a sacrifice]: Isa 53:12 LXX uses anaphero for the Servant bearing the sins of many): apekdechomenois (eagerly waiting — the same word as Rom 8:19, 23; the eschatological expectation-posture of the community awaiting the parousia).

10.1: skian gar echōn ho nomos tōn mellontōn agathōn, ouk autēn tēn eikona tōn pragmatōn — "for since the law has but a shadow of the good things to come instead of the true form of these realities" (skian echōn: having/holding a shadow — the shadow is the law's characteristic possession, not an accidental quality; ouk autēn tēn eikona: not the image itself — the law has a shadow but not the eikona [image, form, replica] of the things): the distinction between skia (shadow) and eikōn (image) is that the shadow lacks the substance and clarity of the image — both are less than the reality, but the image is a truer representation than the shadow. The Platonic background of copy-and-original is mediated here through Hellenistic Jewish thought.
10.4: adynaton gar haima taurōn kai tragōn aphairein hamartias — "for it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins" (adynaton: impossible — the same strong impossibility as 6:4; the repeated adynaton is characteristic of Hebrews' categorical claims): the impossibility is ontological, not merely pragmatic — animal blood cannot remove moral guilt because the ontological gap between animal life and human guilt is unbridgeable. The repeated OT sacrifices implicitly acknowledged this — if they had achieved the removal, they would not need repeating (v2).
10.5: sōma de katērtisō moi — "but a body you have prepared for me" (katērtisō: you prepared, fashioned, made ready — perfect active, addressing God): the LXX of Ps 40:6 reads sōma de katērtisō moi where the MT has ōzen koritha li (ears you have dug/opened for me — the idiom for God opening the ears for obedience). The LXX translator may have understood the opened ears as a metonymy for the receptive body (the obedient self), or may have been working from a variant Hebrew text. Hebrews uses the LXX reading to make the incarnation the fulfillment of Ps 40 — the Son comes in the prepared body to do God's will.
10.10: en hō thelēmati hēgiasmenoi esmen dia tēs prosphoras tou sōmatos Iēsou Christou ephapax — "and by that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all" (hēgiasmenoi esmen: we have been sanctified — perfect passive periphrastic: the sanctification is a completed state with present reality; ephapax: once for all — the definitive, non-repeatable character of the offering): the thelemati (by the will) links back to Ps 40:8 ("to do your will, O God") — the Son's will-doing is the mechanism of the community's sanctification. The body-offering that replaced the animal-sacrifices is the will-doing that the Psalm anticipated.
10.11: kai pas men hiereus hestēken kath' hēmeran leitourgōn kai tas autas pollakis prospherōn thysias, haitines oudepote dynantai perielein hamartias — "and every priest stands daily at his service, offering repeatedly the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins" (hestēken: stands — the standing priest is the image of unfinished work; contrast with the seated Son of v12): the Levitical priests' standing is not incidental but structural — there were no chairs in the sanctuary, because the work was never done. The contrast with the seated Christ (who sat down after offering one sacrifice for sins forever) makes the posture theologically significant.
10.19: echontes oun, adelphoi, parrēsian eis tēn eisodon tōn hagiōn en tō haimati Iēsou — "therefore, brothers and sisters, since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus" (parrēsian: confidence, bold access, freedom of speech/entry — in the classical world: the citizen's right to speak in the assembly; in this context: the right to enter the sanctuary that was previously restricted): eisodon (entry, access) — the parrēsia is specifically an entry-right, not only an emotional confidence. The blood of Jesus is the basis of the access right, as the high priest's blood was the basis of his Day of Atonement access.
10.23: katechōmen tēn homologian tēs elpidos aklinē, pistos gar ho epangeilamenos — "let us hold fast the confession of our hope without wavering, for he who promised is faithful" (katechōmen: let us hold down, hold fast — same verb as 3:6, 14: the community's grip on what was received; homologian: confession — same word as 3:1; the community's public declaration of faith; aklinē: without bending, without inclining — rigid against pressure): the reason to hold fast is divine faithfulness, not human effort — the community holds its grip because the one who promised is trustworthy.
10.25: mē enkataleipontes tēn episynagōgēn heautōn, kathōs ethos tisin, alla parakalontes, kai tosoutō mallon hosō blepete engizousan tēn hēmeran — "not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another, and all the more as you see the Day drawing near" (episynagōgēn: assembling together — a compound of syn- + agōgē: the bringing-together of the group; also used in 2 Thess 2:1 for the eschatological gathering): the present tense participle indicates ongoing neglect by some within the community. Hēmeran (the Day): the eschatological Day of the Lord/Christ's return, whose proximity motivates intensified mutual encouragement.
10.26: hekousiōs gar hamartanontōn hēmōn meta to labein tēn epignōsin tēs alētheias, ouketi peri hamartias apoleipetai thysia — "for if we go on sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" (hekousiōs: willingly, voluntarily, deliberately — the contrast with the Levitical atonement of inadvertent sins [9:7, agnoēmatōn]; the deliberate, willful rejection of Christ's sacrifice leaves no atonement mechanism available): epignōsin tēs alētheias (the full knowledge of the truth) — this is not ordinary ignorance but knowledge-with-recognition of what has been received.
10.31: phoberon to empesein eis cheiras theou zōntos — "it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God" (phoberon: fearful, terrifying — predicate adjective; empesein eis cheiras: to fall into the hands of — the idiom of helpless subjection to a superior power): the formulation inverts the Psalter's comfort-language of "falling into God's hands" as divine mercy (2 Sam 24:14 = falling into God's hands rather than human hands). Here the falling is judgment rather than mercy — the distinction being the attitude of the one who falls.
10.38: ho de dikaios mou ek pisteōs zēsetai, kai ean hyposteilētai, ouk eudokei hē psychē mou en autō — "but my righteous one shall live by faith, and if he shrinks back, my soul has no pleasure in him" (ek pisteōs zēsetai: will live from/out of faith — the same Hab 2:4 citation as in Rom 1:17 and Gal 3:11, but with different emphasis: Hebrews adds the negative counterpart "if he shrinks back"): hyposteilētai (shrinks back — aorist subjunctive of hypostellō: to draw back, shorten sails, retreat): the nautical imagery again — shrinking back is the failure to hold one's position, the pulling-back of the one who was moving forward.

11.1: Estin de pistis elpizomenōn hypostasis, pragmatōn elenchos ou blepomenōn — "now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen" (hypostasis: substance, underlying reality, foundation — the same word as 1:3 [exact imprint of his hypostasis] and 3:14 [if we hold the beginning of our hypostasis]): in Greek philosophy hypostasis referred to the underlying reality beneath appearances; here faith is described as the hypostasis of the hoped-for things — not hope itself but the substantiality, the made-real quality, that faith gives to what is hoped for. Elenchos: proof, demonstration, conviction — a legal/logical term for evidence that compels belief: faith is the demonstration of unseen realities, not a subjective state of optimism.
11.3: Pistei noumen katērtisthai tous aiōnas rhēmati theou, eis to mē ek phainomenōn ta blepomena gegonenai — "by faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible" (katērtisthai tous aiōnas rhēmati theou: the ages were fitted-together/constituted by the word of God — katartizō: to prepare, put in order, constitute): eis to mē ek phainomenōn ta blepomena gegonenai: so that the visible came to be not from visible things — the creation-from-nothing principle (creatio ex nihilo): the visible order does not derive from pre-existing visible material but from the divine word that preceded and generated it.
11.6: chōris de pisteōs adynaton euarestēsai; pisteusai gar dei ton proserchomenon tō theō hoti estin kai tois ekzētousin auton misthapadotēs ginetai — "and without faith it is impossible to please him, for whoever would draw near to God must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him" (adynaton euarestēsai: impossible to please — the third adynaton of Hebrews, establishing faith as the absolute prerequisite for approaching God; tois ekzētousin auton: to those who seek him — present active participle: the seeking is ongoing, not a one-time quest): misthapadotēs: rewarder, one who gives back wages — misthos + apodidōmi; a compound found only here in the NT: God repays the investment of faith-seeking with himself.
11.10: exedecheto gar tēn tous themelious echousan polin, hēs technitos kai dēmiourgos ho theos — "for he was looking forward to the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God" (technitos: craftsman, artisan — one who designs and makes; dēmiourgos: public craftsman, builder — in Plato the Demiurge is the craftsman-god who orders the cosmos): the city with foundations is the opposite of a tent (the patriarchal dwelling) — permanent, foundationed, divinely constructed. Abraham's tent-dwelling was not failure but faith — he lived in the type while seeking the antitype.
11.13: kata pistin apethanon houtoi pantes, mē labontes tas epangelias, alla pōrrothen autas idontes kai aspasamenoi kai homologēsantes hoti xenoi kai parepidēmoi eisin epi tēs gēs — "these all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar, and having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth" (pōrrothen autas idontes: having seen them from far off — the patriarchs' eschatological vision that reached beyond their earthly lifetime; aspasamenoi: having greeted, embraced — the gesture toward something at a distance: a wave, a salute): xenoi kai parepidēmoi: strangers and sojourners/exiles — the technical terms for resident-aliens in Hellenistic usage; the patriarchs self-identify as non-citizens of the present order.
11.24: Pistei Mōysēs megas genomenos ērnēsato legesthai huios thygatros Pharaō — "by faith Moses, when he was grown up, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter" (ērnēsato legesthai: refused to be called — the double verb: refusing + the passive infinitive of naming; it was not a name he bore but a name given by others that he rejected): the faith-act is the refusal of a social identity. Egyptian royal adoption made Moses heir to immense privilege; his refusal is faith's voluntary social descent in solidarity with the covenant people.
11.26: meizona plouton hēgēsamenos tōn Aigyptou thēsaurōn ton oneidismon tou Christou — "he considered the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt" (meizona plouton hēgēsamenos: having considered greater wealth — the deliberate cognitive evaluation of comparative worth; ton oneidismon tou Christou: the reproach of Christ — the identification of Moses's suffering with the Messiah's reproach is the Christological reading of Ps 69:9 and 89:50-51): Moses's reproach is identified with Christ's because it was reproach suffered for the sake of the same covenant promise — a typological solidarity across the testaments.
11.33: hoi dia pisteōs katēgōnisanto basileias, eirgatēsanto dikaiosynēn, epetychon epangeliōn, ephraxan stomata leontōn — "who through faith conquered kingdoms, enforced justice, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions" (katēgōnisanto basileias: conquered kingdoms — the military-athletic verb of wrestling/competing to a finish; the OT judges and kings who won battles): epetychon epangeliōn (obtained promises): not the complete eschatological promise (11:39) but specific historical promises — Gideon's deliverance, David's kingship, etc. Ephraxan stomata leontōn: stopped the mouths of lions — Daniel in the lions' den (Dan 6) and Samson (Judg 14:6).
11.35: hoi de ou prosdexamenoi tēn apolutrōsin, hina kreittōnos anastaseōs tychōsin — "some were tortured, refusing to accept release, so that they might rise again to a better resurrection" (ou prosdexamenoi tēn apolutrōsin: not having accepted the release/redemption — the same apolutrōsis as 9:15 and Eph 1:14; here not the redemptive act but the offer of release from torture): kreittōnos anastaseōs: a better resurrection — not the resuscitation of 11:35a (returning to mortal life) but the eschatological resurrection to immortal life. The faith that refuses earthly rescue for the sake of the coming resurrection is the supreme expression of 11:1's assurance-of-hoped-for-things.
11.39: kai houtoi pantes martyrēthentes dia tēs pisteōs ouk ekomisanto tēn epangelian — "and all these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised" (martyrēthentes: having been witnessed to, testified about, approved — the passive of martyreō: to bear witness; God's testimony about them in Scripture is the divine commendation): ouk ekomisanto tēn epangelian (did not receive the promise — the singular promise): the entire OT community of faith died before the fulfillment; they received historical-provisional fulfillments but not the eschatological completion. Tou theou peri hēmōn kreitton ti problepsamenos (v40: God having provided something better for us) — the new covenant community is the completing context for the OT cloud of witnesses.

12.1: toigaroun kai hēmeis, tosoutos echontes perikeimenon hēmin nephon martyrōn — "therefore, since we are surrounded by so great a cloud of witnesses" (nephon martyrōn: cloud of witnesses — nephos: cloud, mass, great multitude; martyrōn: witnesses — genitive of apposition: a cloud that consists of witnesses): the martyres are witnesses not in the sense of spectators watching the current community's race but witnesses whose lives and deaths were themselves testimonies to faith (the sense of martyreō throughout ch11: to be approved, to testify). They witness by having witnessed — their completed lives are the evidence. Apothemenoi panta ogkon (having laid aside every weight): the athletic image of stripping for the race.
12.2: aphorōntes eis ton tēs pisteōs archēgon kai teleiōtēn Iēsoun — "looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith" (aphorōntes: looking away to, looking off toward — the apo-prefix: looking away from distractions and fixing the gaze on Jesus; archēgon: pioneer/founder — same term as 2:10; teleiōtēn: perfecter/completer — one who brings to the goal): hos anti tēs prokeimenēs autō charas hypemeinen stauron, aischynēs kataphronēsas (who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising shame): anti: instead of or in exchange for; "for the joy" or "instead of the joy" — the ambiguity is between joy that motivated endurance and joy that was set aside for endurance. Both readings are grammatically possible; the former ("motivated by") is more commonly adopted.
12.4: Oupō mechri haimatos antikatesatēsate pros tēn hamartian antagōnizomenoi — "in your struggle against sin you have not yet resisted to the point of shedding blood" (antikatesatēsate: you stood against/resisted — the athletic compound of anti [against] + kata [down] + histēmi [stand]; antagōnizomenoi: struggling against — athletic combat vocabulary): the community has suffered but not yet martyrdom. The "not yet" places their experience below the level of the exemplars of ch11 (the sawn-asunder, the tortured) and certainly below the level of Jesus's cross — calibrating the community's perception of their own suffering.
12.5: kai eklellēsthe tēs parakelseos hētis hymin hōs huiois dialegetai — "and have you forgotten the exhortation that addresses you as sons?" (eklellēsthe: have you forgotten — perfect passive of eklanthanomai: to forget completely; hōs huiois dialegetai: speaks to you as sons — the present tense of ongoing address): the Prov 3:11-12 citation is not merely past encouragement but present address — Scripture continues to speak to the community in its current situation. The forgetting is the spiritual problem; the citation is the remedy.
12.10: hoi men gar pros oligas hēmeras kata to dokoun autois epaideuon, ho de epi to sympheron eis to metalabein tēs hagiōtētos autou — "for they disciplined us for a short time as it seemed best to them, but he disciplines us for our good, that we may share his holiness" (pros to sympheron: for the profitable/beneficial — the sympheron is the beneficial purpose vs. the arbitrary "seemed good to them" of human discipline; metalabein tēs hagiōtētos autou: to partake of/receive a share of his holiness — the goal of divine discipline is holiness-participation): the divine discipline is not arbitrary suffering but purposive formation toward the community's participation in the divine character.
12.11: pasa de paideia pros men to paron ou dokei charas einai alla lypēs, hysteron de karpon eirēnikon tois di' autēs gegymnasmenos apodidōsin dikaiosynēs — "for the moment all discipline seems painful rather than pleasant, but later it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it" (karpon eirēnikon: peaceful fruit — eirēnikon as an adjective modifying the fruit: the fruit has the quality of peace rather than turmoil; gegymnasmenos: having been trained/exercised — from gymnazō: to exercise naked, to train vigorously): dikaiosynēs is either subjective genitive (righteous fruit = the fruit that is righteousness) or qualitative (fruit of a righteous kind).
12.18: Ou gar proselēlythate psēlaphōmenō kai kekaumēnō pyri kai gnophō kai zophō kai thyellē — "for you have not come to what may be touched, a blazing fire and darkness and gloom and a tempest" (psēlaphōmenō: that may be touched, handled — perfect passive participle of psēlaphaō: to touch, feel, grope; the touchability of Sinai marks its earthly-material character; kekaumēnō pyri: blazing fire — perfect passive: fire that had been set burning and remains burning): the entire Sinai list is characterized as sensory, earthly, terrifying — a deliberate phenomenological contrast with the heavenly assembly of vv22-24.
12.22: alla proselēlythate Siōn orei kai polei theou zōntos, Ierousalēm epouraniō, kai myriasi angelōn, panēgyrei — "but you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to innumerable angels in festal gathering" (myriasi angelōn: myriads of angels — ten-thousands, uncountable; panēgyrei: festal gathering/assembly — the great civic or religious assembly for celebration): the distinction between panēgyrei (festal gathering of angels) and ekklēsia prōtotokōn (the assembly of the firstborn) has been debated: are these one group or two? Most take them as two separate groups within the heavenly city. Prōtotokōn (firstborn, plural): those who share the firstborn's inheritance — the community's heavenly enrollment is in the book of the firstborn-status believers.
12.24: kai diathēkēs neas mesitē Iēsou kai haimati rhantismou kreitton lalounti para ton Abel — "and to Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel" (diathēkēs neas: new covenant — here neas [numerically new, recent] rather than the kainēs [qualitatively new] of 8:8, 13 and 9:15; the variation may be for stylistic variety or to emphasize the covenant's temporal recency): haimati rhantismou (blood of sprinkling) — the OT background of the Mosaic covenant's inaugurating blood-sprinkling (Exod 24:8; Heb 9:19-22), now applied to Christ's blood. Kreitton lalounti para ton Abel: speaking better than Abel — Abel's blood cried for justice; Christ's blood speaks forgiveness.
12.28: dio basileian asaleuton paralambanontes echōmen charin, di' hēs latreuōmen euarestōs tō theō meta eulabeias kai deous — "therefore let us be grateful for receiving a kingdom that cannot be shaken, and thus let us offer to God acceptable worship, with reverence and awe" (asaleuton: unshakeable, unable to be shaken — the negation of the saleusō from Hag 2:6 cited in v26): the unshakeable kingdom received through the Christological fulfillment of Haggai's shaking-promise is the basis of the community's gratitude-worship. Eulabeias kai deous: reverence and awe — the two terms of the appropriate human posture before the consuming-fire God (v29).
12.29: kai gar ho theos hēmōn pyr katanaliskōn — "for our God is a consuming fire" (pyr katanaliskōn: consuming fire — citing Deut 4:24 LXX exactly): the closing verse applies the divine consuming-fire character to the new covenant community's worship-life. The God who is consuming fire is not abandoned in the new covenant but approached with greater access — the approach requires the greater reverence that the Son's blood makes possible and necessary.

13.2: tēs philoxenias mē epilanthanese, dia tautēs gar elathon tines xenisontes angelous — "do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels without knowing it" (philoxenias: love-of-stranger, hospitality — philos + xenos: affection toward the foreigner; elathon: escaped notice, were unaware — aorist of lanthanō: to escape notice): the construction elathon... xenisontes (escaped notice while showing hospitality to) is the idiom for doing something without realizing it. The Genesis precedent (Abraham and Lot) establishes that ordinary hospitality can be divine encounter.
13.7: mnēmoneuete tōn hēgoumenōn hymōn, hoitines elalēsan hymin ton logon tou theou, hōn anatheoroyntes tēn ekbasin tēs anastrophēs, mimeisthe tēn pistin — "remember your leaders, those who spoke to you the word of God. Consider the outcome of their way of life, and imitate their faith" (hēgoumenōn: leaders, those who lead — the present active participle as a title: those who exercise leadership): the specific "outcome" (ekbasis: exit, outcome, result — cf. 1 Cor 10:13) of the leaders' lives suggests these are leaders who have died — possibly martyrs whose death-pattern the community is to contemplate and emulate. Mimeisthe tēn pistin: imitate their faith — the faith-quality is the object of imitation, not specific actions.
13.8: Iēsous Christos echthes kai sēmeron ho autos kai eis tous aiōnas — "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever" (echthes: yesterday; sēmeron: today; eis tous aiōnas: unto the ages, forever): the temporal statement covers past-present-future in one compact predication. In context (after the call to remember past leaders and before the warning against strange teachings), the unchangeableness of Christ grounds the community's resistance to doctrinal novelty — what was taught by the past leaders under the eternal Christ remains valid under the same eternal Christ. The verse is not a general statement of divine immutability but a contextual anchor against theological drift.
13.10: echomen thysiastērion ex hou phagein ouk echousin exousian hoi tē skēnē latreuontes — "we have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat" (thysiastērion: altar — the place of sacrifice; exousian phagein: right/authority to eat): the altar-eating privilege was a genuine feature of the Levitical system: the priests ate portions of certain sacrifices. The community's altar is the cross; the eating is participation in the benefits of Christ's sacrifice (cf. 1 Cor 10:18 on the relationship between eating and altar-participation). Those who maintain the tent-system forfeit the right to eat from the Christological altar.
13.12: dio kai Iēsous, hina hagiasē dia tou idiou haimatos ton laon, exō tēs pylēs epathen — "so Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood" (exō tēs pylēs: outside the gate — the Jerusalem city gate; the crucifixion site outside the city walls is the typological fulfillment of the atonement-animals burned outside the camp): hina hagiasē... ton laon (that he might sanctify the people): the purpose of the outside-the-gate suffering is the sanctification of the people — the same purpose as the burning of the sin-offering outside the camp. The spatial dislocation of the sacrifice is the mechanism of the community's purification.
13.15: di' autou oun anapherōmen thysian aineseōs dia pantos tō theō, tout' estin karpon cheiloōn homologountōn tō onomati autou — "through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name" (anapherōmen thysian aineseōs: let us offer up a sacrifice of praise — anapherō: to carry up, to bring to the altar; the same verb used for the Aaronic offerings [5:1] and for Christ bearing sins [9:28]): karpon cheiloōn homologountōn (fruit of lips that confess/acknowledge): Hos 14:2 LXX uses karpon cheiloōn (fruit of lips) as the replacement for animal sacrifice after judgment — Hebrews inherits this prophetic substitution and grounds it in Christ's completed sacrifice.
13.20: Ho de theos tēs eirēnēs, ho anagagōn ek nekrōn ton poimena tōn probatōn ton megan en haimati diathēkēs aiōniou, ton kyrion hēmōn Iēsoun — "now may the God of peace who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, the great shepherd of the sheep, by the blood of the eternal covenant" (anagagōn ek nekrōn: having brought up from the dead — anagō: to bring up/lead up; the unusual verb choice [not the standard egeirō = raise] recalls the Shepherd-from-the-sea imagery of Isa 63:11; the bringing-up from death echoes the bringing-up from Egypt): en haimati diathēkēs aiōniou: by/in the blood of the eternal covenant — the resurrection is grounded in the eternal covenant sealed by blood; the blood precedes and grounds the resurrection. Ton poimena ton megan: the great shepherd — the superlative characterization of the risen Christ as shepherd fulfills the OT shepherd-messiah hope (Ezek 34:23-24; 37:24).
